segments.s715-g33: بنزد is the archaic prepositional phrase 'به نزد' (to the presence of) placed before the pronoun; تو is the 2nd‑singular addressee. آمد is the simple past 'came'. بپذرفتیش is a past tense verb with the enclitic object -ش ('him/it') and 2nd‑person subject implied: 'you accepted him.'
segments.s715-g34: چو is the temporal 'when/as'. پر here functions as 'full (of)' modifying the following noun, so پر مایگان = 'full of mâyegân / a crowd of beings/attendants'. دست is the noun 'hand' and بگرفتیش is another past verb with enclitic -ش: literally '(you) seized/took him' (the subject is omitted or supplied from context).
segments.s715-g35: This is a rhetorical question. کس is the indefinite 'anyone' (expecting a negative answer). این راه is 'this way/course'. برگیرد is a classical present/subjunctive or potential form 'would take up/assume'; از راستان means 'from the righteous' or 'among the upright' (از = from/of). Word order follows classical Persian question style: subject/indefinite → object → verb → adjunct.
segments.s715-g36: نیم here expresses negation in a terse, archaic way (effectively 'I am not'); this sense is not in the supplied list. من is the subject 'I'. ب + دین corresponds to بدین 'by/with this' (the b- prefix = 'to/with'); کار is 'affair/matter'. هم‌داستان is a predicate adjective 'of the same mind / in agreement'. The clause means 'I am not in agreement about this matter.'
segments.s715-g37: چو is the temporal 'when'. این نامه is the direct object 'this letter'. آرند is a classical present plural form of آوردن ('they bring/bring near'); near/direction follows: نزدیک تو = 'near you'. The verb comes before the adverbial phrase, as is common in older Persian.
segments.s715-g38: پر here is an adverbialized adjective 'full' → 'with much' (i.e., 'give much thought'). اندیشه کن is an imperative construction: اندیشه (thought) + کن (do) → 'think/ponder!'. رای تاریک تو is a noun phrase 'your dark counsel/plan' where تاریک (adjective) follows رای (noun) and تو is the postposed possessive 'your'.
segments.s715-g39: گر is the conditional 'if'. آن بنده را uses را as the definite/object marker: 'that servant (as object)'. پای کرده is a verbal adjective/participle combination literally 'having made/secured the feet' (i.e. 'having bound his feet' or 'fixing his feet'); کرده is the perfect participle 'done'. ببند is an imperative 'bind/fasten'. The clause issues a conditional command: 'If (you) secure/bind that servant's feet...' (the sequence of participle + imperative is a compact instruction).
segments.s715-g40: فرستی is the 2nd‑person present/subjunctive form of 'to send' used in the protasis ('if you send'). بر ما شوی is a result clause where بر = 'to/toward' and شوی is the present/subjunctive/2nd‑person of شدن 'become' → 'you will become/you become to us'. سودمند is the predicate adjective 'useful/profitable'. Together: 'if you send (him) to us, you will prove useful to us.'
segments.s715-g25: This clause begins with یکی ('a certain'), introducing an indefinite person. بنده بد ('bad servant') is noun + adjective in normal order; شاه را uses the object/definite marker را to mark 'servant of the king' as a definite/particular relation. ناسپاس is a predicative adjective 'ungrateful' describing that servant. The whole clause is a nominal description (no separate finite verb here).
segments.s715-g26: The correlative negation نه ... و نه ... is implicit here (the initial نه applies to both halves). مهتر شناس and یزدان‌شناس are compound adjectival/agentive forms where -شناس ('knower/one who knows') attaches to a noun: 'one who knows/respects the chief' and 'one who knows God' (i.e. pious). و simply joins the two negatives.
segments.s715-g27: یکی again introduces an indefinite person. Here خرد is used adjectivally in the sense 'small/insignificant' rather than the abstract noun 'wisdom'. The adjectives are coordinated with و: 'insignificant and idle and nameless.' بود is the past copula 'was' completing the predication.
segments.s715-g28: پدر بر کشیدش is a verbal phrase: پدر 'father' is the subject; بر + کشید (a prefixed/compound sense 'lift/raise') plus the enclitic object pronoun -ش 'him' gives 'his father raised him'. که introduces the subordinate clause giving the reason/time ('because/for that'): هنگام بود 'it was the time' with بود as the copula.
segments.s715-g29: نهان نیست is a negated predicate: نهان 'hidden' + نیست 'is not'. کردار او 'his deed(s)' is the noun + pronoun; در جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world.' Persian places the adjectival predicate before the copula here.
segments.s715-g30: میان ... و میان ... repeats the preposition میان ('between/among') for poetic parallelism. کهان is used here in the sense 'the ancients' (or ancient ones) and مهان as 'guests/visitors' — both are objects of میان, coordinated by و.
segments.s715-g31: کس here functions negatively as 'no one' (کس ... نپذرفت = 'no one accepted him'). او را is the object marked by را; نپذرفت is the past negative of 'to accept/receive' (ن- + پذیرفت). کش مایه بود is a nominal predicate: کش functions as an modifying/compound element and مایه 'means/resource' with بود 'was' yields 'he had no resources' or 'he was of low material/means' — a short statement of his lack of support.
segments.s715-g32: وگر introduces a concessive/hypothetical clause 'but/and if'. در خرد 'in wisdom' uses خرد as the abstract noun 'wisdom'; برترین پایه 'the highest base/pedestal' is a superlative phrase with برترین modifying پایه. بود is the copula 'was' — the clause means 'even if he were of the highest rank in wisdom.'
segments.s715-g81: چنین (an adverb) precedes the verb to mean 'thus/so'. یافت is the past tense of یافْتن (to find/receive); the subject is implicit (they/people mentioned earlier). پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply', and ز ایرانیان is a poetic prepositional phrase (ز = از) meaning 'from the Iranians'.
segments.s715-g82: که‌ای (here introducing reported speech) functions like the conjunction 'that/that is' introducing what the Iranians said. The following nouns are a coordinated list: فر (the concept/name 'farr'—royal glory), اورند (a proper name), و joins items, and تاج کیان is a genitival phrase 'the crown of the Kians/kings'.
segments.s715-g83: چنین modifies کارها ('such matters'); the prepositional phrase بر دل ('upon the heart') gives the target. آسان is an adjective meaning 'easy/light', and مگیر is the negative imperative of گرفتن ('do not take'). Word order places the adverbial/item before the prohibition: 'Do not take such affairs upon the heart.'
segments.s715-g84: یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one/once'. رای is used as 'counsel/plan' here, and زن is an imperative forming a light-verb construction (رأی زدن) meaning 'take/offer counsel' or 'deliberate'—so رای زن = 'consult/seek counsel'. با introduces the person with whom to consult, خردمند پیر = 'the wise old man'.
segments.s715-g85: به نامه is a prepositional phrase 'in the letter' or 'write in the letter'. چنین کار آسان is the phrase 'such a matter (made) easy/light', and مکن is the prohibitive imperative 'do not'. Together it means 'Write in the letter: do not treat such a matter lightly.'
segments.s715-g86: مکن is again the negative imperative 'do not'. تیره is an adjective 'dark/bleak' used as the quality one must not bring about; این qualifies the following nouns ('this'), فر 'farr/royal glory' and شمع کهن 'the ancient lamp' are the objects being protected from being darkened or dimmed. Word order places the negative verb first for emphasis ('Do not — darken this farr and the old lamp').
segments.s715-g87: گزین کن is the imperative 'choose' (گزین plus the helper imperative کن). از ایران is the source phrase 'from Iran'. یکی مرد پیر is the indefinite noun phrase 'one old man' (numeral + noun + adjective).
segments.s715-g88: This segment lists attributes joined by و: خردمند 'wise' and زیبا 'handsome/beautiful' and گرد (an epithet here; classical Persian uses گرد in honorific senses) and دبیر 'scribe/secretary'. The adjectives are coordinated qualities describing the man chosen.
segments.s715-g1: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' introducing the outcome. تا here is a temporal conjunction 'when/once' (classical usage) linking the clause that follows. خبرها is the plural subject 'the reports/news'. به ایران رسید is a verbal phrase: به + place + رسید (past of رسیدن) = 'reached Iran'. Word order is Subject — (to-place) — Verb, typical in Persian.
segments.s715-g2: بر here is a preposition meaning 'to/toward' (classical usage 'بر ... رسید' = 'came to ...'). پادشاه دلیران is a genitive phrase 'the king of the brave' (دلیران = 'the brave ones'); note the possessor/head order typical in Persian poetry. رسید is the same verb 'arrived/reached' agreeing with the plural/collective news implicit in the prior clause.
segments.s715-g3: که introduces a subordinate or explanatory clause ('that'). بهرام را uses the enclitic را as a topical/emphatic marker ('as for Bahram'); in classical Persian possession is often expressed by placing the possessed things before the copula, so پادشاهی و گنج are the things associated with Bahram. The phrase as a whole states that 'kingship and treasure are (with) Bahram.'
segments.s715-g4: This segment is compact and slightly elliptical. ازان literally 'of/from that' sets up a comparison/partitive ('of those'), تو بیش است reads as a comparative ('more is yours' or 'yours is greater') and است is the copula. نابرده رنج is a verbless adjectival phrase meaning 'having not borne toil' (a classical passive/perfective participial idea expressed without an explicit auxiliary) and functions as a qualification of 'you' (i.e. 'you, who have not endured hardship'). The word order and omission of a linking verb are typical of poetic diction.
segments.s715-g5: پراز درد و غم شد is a predicate meaning 'became full of pain and sorrow' (پراز = 'full of', شد = 'became'). ز تیمار اوی is a causal prepositional phrase 'from/because of his care/solicitude' (ز = 'from/because of', تیمار = 'care', اوی = possessive 'his'); note the possessive follows the noun in this older form.
segments.s715-g6: دلش is 'his heart' formed by noun + enclitic ش (his). گشت is past of گشتن used as 'became/turned'. پیچان is an adjectival/state word 'twisting/twisted' describing the heart's condition. ز کردار اوی = 'because of his deeds/conduct' with ز marking cause and اوی the possessive 'his'. The clause thus gives the resultant state (دلش گشت پیچان) and its cause (ز کردار اوی).
segments.s715-g7: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'continually/kept on'. رای زد is an idiom: رای (counsel) + زد (past of زدن) — here زدن is a light verb forming the verbal expression 'to deliberate/take counsel'. با بزرگان 'with the great ones' indicates company. بهم (بهم) emphasizes mutual/together action ('together/one with another'). Note the poetic order and light-verb construction.
segments.s715-g8: بسی گفت means 'much was said' or 'he said a great deal' (بسی = 'much'). و انداخت is 'and he cast/laid out' — انداخت from انداختن used here idiomatically as 'to set forth, to consider/put forward'. از بیش و کم is a partitive/contrastive phrase 'of the greater and lesser' i.e. 'pros and cons' or 'in all respects'. The sequence expresses that he discussed extensively and considered both major and minor points.
segments.s715-g89: کز begins a relative clause 'who/that'; ایدر = 'here' (an older/dialectal locative). The phrase به نزدیک خاقان شود literally places the direction/goal (به نزدیک خاقان = 'to/near the Khaghan') before the verb شود ('becomes/comes'), so شود is a third-person verbal form meaning 'approaches' or 'becomes near' (classical narrative/habitual sense).
segments.s715-g90: سخن گوید = 'he speaks' (سخن = 'speech'); گوید here functions as the reporting/speaking verb (classical present/narrative), though the provided sense list does not include the verb 'say'. و links two coordinated predicates. راه او بشنود = 'hears/listens to his way/advice' with راه ('way, counsel') as the object and بشنود (present/hears) as the verb; word order is object before verb, subject is understood from context.
segments.s715-g91: بگوید که introduces reported speech/description ('he says/relates that'). بگوید is used as a narrative/present reporting verb here. که is the clausal introducer 'that'; the following noun phrase بهرام روز نخست ('Bahram, the first day') gives the temporal frame 'on the first day (concerning Bahram)'.
segments.s715-g92: که بود = 'who he was' with که as relative 'who/that' and بود as past copula 'was'. و links a second clause: پس از پهلوانی (the prepositional phrase 'after his heroism/after becoming a hero') followed by چه جست ('what he sought') — چه is the interrogative/indefinite 'what' and جست is the past of جستن 'to seek'. The verb appears at the clause end in typical Persian order.
segments.s715-g93: همی بود is an archaic/poetic progressive/continuous 'it was/it continued so' (همی adds durative force). تا introduces a temporal boundary 'until'. کار او گشت راست literally 'his affair became right/straight' — گشت is the past of گشتن 'to become/turn', and راست is the predicate 'right/settled'. Word order places the verb after its complement.
segments.s715-g94: خداوند را uses را as a topicalizer/emphasis marker here (not a strict object marker), so 'the Lord' is presented as the topic/agent. زان سپس = 'thereupon/after that' (زان = 'from/for that', سپس = 'then'). Main clause بنده خواست = 'sought/wanted a servant' with خواست past 'wanted'. Overall: 'The Lord, thereupon, sought a servant.'
segments.s715-g95: چو here functions as a conditional 'if'. نیکو نگردد is a negative present/subjunctive/passive sense 'is not made/grows good' (نگردد = 'does not become/turn into good'). به یک ماه‌کار is an adverbial measure phrase 'in/with one month's work' (به + numeral + noun marking extent/duration).
segments.s715-g96: تمامی = 'entirely/wholly' (adverbial). بسالی = 'in a year' (expressing extent or duration). برد is the main verb (from بردن 'to take/carry'); the provided sense list does not include this sense, so -1 is used. روزگار = 'time/the course of time' (the agent here). The clause means roughly 'time will take [it] a whole year.'
segments.s715-g49: همی‌بود + past verb is a classical Persian progressive/habitual past construction: همی‌بود here signals a continuing action (‘kept on’) and is followed by تا introducing the limit-point. تا means ‘until’ and governs the clause that ends with the past verb بدید (archaic form of دید). شمع رخشان is a noun phrase ‘radiant lamp/candle’ with the adjective رخشان after the noun, a common poetic order.
segments.s715-g50: به here marks direction/location ‘toward/into’. درگاه خاقان is a genitive phrase ‘the court/threshold of the khagan’; چینی qualifies خاقان as ‘Chinese’. دوید is the simple past ‘ran/hastened’ and comes at the end of the clause, completing the motion expressed by the prepositional phrase.
segments.s715-g51: بیاورد is a past third-person singular ‘(he) brought’ or ‘(he) caused to be brought.’ خاقان is the subject; هم آنگه are adverbial modifiers (هم = ‘also/too’, آنگه = ‘then’), placed before the object دبیر (a secretary/scribe) by poetic inversion. The verb appears before or after subject in poetry depending on rhythm; here the verb is fronted.
segments.s715-g52: This is a simple coordinated list of items brought: ابا (classical aba, a cloak/outer garment), خامه ‘quill/pen’, مشک ‘musk’, و joins items, and چینی حریر ‘Chinese silk’ (adjective چینی before noun حریر). The whole phrase is the direct object(s) associated with بیاورد.
segments.s715-g53: به پاسخ نوشت is a light prepositional phrase + past verb: به پاسخ ‘in (answer) to the reply’ and نوشت ‘he wrote’. آفرین here functions as a noun ‘praise/acclamation’ rather than the verb ‘to create’, and نهان is adverbial/qualifying ‘secretly’ or ‘hidden’, placed after the noun for emphasis in poetic diction.
segments.s715-g54: ز here is the poetic preposition ‘from’. من بنده is a humble self-reference ‘I, (your) servant’ (بنده used as modest first-person epithet). بر takes the sense ‘toward/unto’ and introduces the beneficiary/recipient phrase کردگار جهان ‘the Lord/Creator of the world’ (کردگار = God, جهان = world).
segments.s715-g55: دگر ‘again/then’ opens the reported clause; گفت is the past-tense reporting verb ‘he said’ (here introducing reported speech or summary). کان is an archaic demonstrative/relative (‘that’ / ‘which’) pointing to نامه ‘the letter’. بر خواندم (written as برخواندم) is a past-tense verb meaning ‘I read aloud / I read out’ (بر‑خواندن used for reading or publicly reciting), reporting that the speaker read the letter.
segments.s715-g56: فرستاده را is the definite object/accusative/topic (‘the messenger’ marked by را). پیش here means ‘in the presence of / before’ (i.e. placed before the court/sovereign). بنشاندم is the past first-person ‘I seated (him)’ or ‘I caused to sit’, completing the reported actions.
segments.s715-g97: "چو" is a temporal conjunction meaning "when." The clause is S (بهرام) + predicate (داماد خاقان بود) where "داماد خاقان" is a predicate noun phrase meaning "the khaghan's son‑in‑law" (the ezāfe is omitted in poetic style) and "بود" is the past copula "was."
segments.s715-g98: "ازو" (= از او) is an oblique pronoun meaning "of/from him" and marks the target of the following verbal noun. "بد سرودن" is an infinitive/verbal noun construction meaning "to sing/compose/speak ill" (بد modifies سرودن). "نه آسان بود" negates the adjective "آسان" with "نه" and finishes with the past copula "بود" — together, "was not easy."
segments.s715-g99: "به خوبی" is an adverbial phrase "in a good/proper way." "سخن گفت" is the verbal phrase "to speak" (سخن + گفت, with گفت used as the verbal element), and "باید" is the impersonal/modal "one must/it is necessary." The final "بسی" (here meaning "sufficiently / much") modifies the necessity expressed by "باید." Note the poetically flexible word order: object/phrase before the verb and modal.
segments.s715-g100: "نهانی" means "hidden/secret(ly)" and here functions as the quality that must not obtain. "نباید" is the negated impersonal modal "must not/should not." "که" introduces the content clause "that ...," and "داند" (classical present/subjunctive form) = "knows" — so the clause says it must not be secret in such a way that "کسی" (anyone) knows. The sequence gives a prohibition against concealment which would allow anyone to discover the matter.
segments.s715-g57: This phrase is archaically written as one word (توبا) but parses as تو با — 'you, with' or 'to (the)'; بندگان is the indirect object 'servants/subjects'; گوی is an imperative 'say' (classicalگو/گوی) addressing the addressee; زین سان سخن is the object phrase 'such speech/words' (زین سان = 'in this manner', سخن = 'speech'). Word order is imperative + indirect object + object phrase (you tell-to servants such words).
segments.s715-g58: نزیبد is the negated present of زیبیدن meaning 'to befit / to suit' and here means 'is not fitting' (3rd person singular); its logical subject is the preceding noun phrase 'زین سان سخن' (such words). از آن خاندان کهن is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'for/of that ancient house' where از marks 'concerning / belonging to'. The clause thus states that such speech does not become that old lineage.
segments.s715-g59: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; مه را ندارند is a straightforward present negation 'they do not have the moon' where مه (literally 'moon') is used metaphorically for radiance/glory and را is the object marker; یکسر 'entirely/at all' intensifies the negation; به مه is a prepositional phrase reinforcing the comparison 'of the moon' (i.e. any moonlike quality).
segments.s715-g60: نه introduces the second coordinated negative clause 'nor'; که را is an object-like construction meaning 'whom' (که + را), with شناسند the verb 'they know/recognize' (3rd person plural); بر جای که literally 'on/in the place that' functions as 'who occupies that place' (i.e. they do not recognize who is in the post/position). The clause therefore says they neither possess the moon's radiance nor recognize who holds the position.
segments.s715-g61: همه چین و توران = 'all (of) China and Turan' (proper-place names follow the quantifier). سراسر 'throughout/entirely' modifies the possessed domain. مراست here is a poetic contraction of 'مرا است' = 'is mine/belongs to me' (copula attached to possessive), so the whole clause asserts dominion over those lands.
segments.s715-g62: به هیتال بر = 'to/over Hital' (هیتال is a personal/place name); نیز 'also' links this clause to the previous boast; فرمان رواست is a compound predication: فرمان (order/decree) + رواست (rava-st) where روا 'fitting' here helps form the phrase 'فرمان‌رواست' meaning 'he rules by command' or 'is sovereign by decree' with ست as the copula 'is'.
segments.s715-g63: نیم تا is an adverbial phrase 'not even for a moment / not a whit' (literally 'half/one half until'); بدم here functions idiomatically ('I would say/call')—in classical diction this is the speaker's statement about what he would call someone; مرد پیمان‌شکن is the noun phrase 'a man who breaks oaths' (object/complement). The clause expresses refusal to brand anyone as faithless even briefly.
segments.s715-g64: تو با من = 'to me / with me' (addressing the interlocutor); چنین داستانها = 'such stories/tales' (چنین 'such', داستانها plural 'stories'); مزن is the negative imperative 'do not tell/do not relate' (مزن here is a prohibitive verb telling the addressee to refrain). The clause is a direct request/order: do not recount such stories to me.
segments.s715-g9: شب تیره is an adverbial time phrase ('on the dark night'); فرمود is a past-tense verb here meaning 'he commanded/ordered' and takes the subordinate clause introduced by تا ('that/so that'); شد is the past of شدن 'to become' and functions here as 'be made/was made', with دبیر as the predicate noun 'scribe'. The clause literally reads 'on the dark night he commanded that (he) be made scribe.'
segments.s715-g10: This is a causative/constructive clause of the pattern 'X را کرد Y' = 'he made X Y'. سرخامه (the pen-nib) is the definite object marked by را, کرد is the past 'made/caused', and پیکان تیر is the predicate nominal 'an arrow (point/shaft)'. Literally: 'He made the pen-nib an arrow.'
segments.s715-g11: به introduces the indirect object 'to' (به خاقان چینی = 'to the Chinese Khagan'); یکی نامه ('a certain letter' or 'one letter') is the direct object; کرد is the past verb 'did/made' used here in the sense 'wrote/sent' (common usage: نامه کردن = to write/send a letter). Word order is preposition + recipient + object + verb.
segments.s715-g12: تو گفتی که is the reporting clause 'you said that...'; که introduces the reported content. از خنجرش خامه کرد is the reported clause: از marks material/source ('from/out of his dagger' — خنجرش = 'his dagger'), خامه 'a quill/pen', and کرد 'made' (i.e. 'he made a pen from his dagger'). Possessive -ش attaches to خنجر.
segments.s715-g13: نخست 'first' is an adverbial; آفرین کرد is an idiom meaning 'offered praise' (آفرین as 'acclamation' and کرد 'did/uttered'); بر is the preposition 'to/upon/for' and کردگار is the object 'the Creator/God'. The verb کرد takes آفرین as its direct object in this idiomatic use.
segments.s715-g14: توانا و دانا are adjectival epithets ('the Powerful and the Wise') applied to کردگار; the coordinating و links them. و به روزگار attaches an additional prepositional object 'and to Time/Fate' (به = 'to/for', روزگار = 'time/fate'), so the whole phrase lists recipients of the praise.
segments.s715-g15: برازنده is an adjective/noun meaning 'befitting' or 'worthy (of)'; it governs the following coordinated list: هور و کیوان و ماه (sun/splendour, Saturn, and moon). The enumerated celestial bodies are the things of which something is said to be 'worthy'.
segments.s715-g16: نشاننده is a participial/noun form 'signifying/indicator'; شاه is the noun it points to ('the king'); بر پیش گاه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before the presence' or 'in the royal presence/at the court' (پیش + گاه forming 'presence, court'). Together: 'a signifier/emblem of the king before the court.'
segments.s715-g65: چو is a conditional/temporal conjunction here meaning “if.” The clause places the object phrase دستِ بهرام before the verb; گیرم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive used for a conditional/future sense (“I take” / “I should take”). بدست follows the verb to mark the manner/location (“in my hand”).
segments.s715-g66: وزان پس is a connective meaning “and/from that, then,” linking this clause to the previous one. به مهر is an instrumental phrase “by/with love/affection” (به used instrumentally). اندرم is اندر + ـم, “in me” (the enclitic −م = “me”). آرم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive/poetic form (“I shall be / I will rest”), and شکست is a noun (“defeat/breaking”) functioning as the resulting state — together indicating “I shall be undone/broken within.”
segments.s715-g67: نخواند is negative 3rd‑person verb (ن‑ + خواند) meaning “will not call/summon / does not call.” مرا is the object “me” (here split in the token list; the clitic marks the first‑person object) and را is the definite/object marker attached in poetry. از آب پاک is a prepositional phrase “by/from pure water” indicating means or rite.
segments.s715-g68: جز از پاک ایزد is an exception phrase “except from the Pure God.” مرا نیست باک is an idiomatic negative existential: مِرا (me; split across tokens) + نیست “there is not / there is no” + باک “fear,” yielding “there is no fear for me / I do not fear (anyone) except the Pure God.” The word order (object before نیست) is typical of classical Persian inversion.
segments.s715-g69: تو را گر (here combined as را‌گر) is a topical/dative + conditional particle: تو را = “to you,” گر = “if.” بزرگی is the noun “greatness” (the thing hypothetically added). بیفزایدی is 2nd‑person singular subjunctive/optative of افزودن/فزودن (“be added / increase”), giving a conditional translation “if greatness were added to you.”
segments.s715-g70: خرد بیشتر = “more wisdom” (خرد = wisdom, بیشتر = more). زین is an archaic/comparative particle here meaning “than this / of this” (classical usage; it is not the common noun ‘saddle’). بدی = “this evil/harm.” شایدی is a modal/optative addressing the addressee (“you might / perhaps you would”), so the clause says you might be wiser than this evil if the prior condition held.
segments.s715-g71: بران = “thereupon / on that.” نامه‌بر = “letter‑carrier / envoy.” مهر بنهاد is a light verbal phrase: مهر here is the object “seal” (or “affection” used as a seal) and بنهاد is past 3rd‑person ‘he placed/put (on it)’; و گفت = “and said.” Word order (adverbial + agent + verb) reflects poetic economy.
segments.s715-g72: که introduces reported speech “that.” با باد here is idiomatic: باد functions as an optative particle (“may”), so the phrase carries an optative/benedictive force; باید “must/ought” plus که باشید (subjunctive “that you be”) gives obligation/desiderative force, and جفت = “pair / mate.” Altogether the reported speech means roughly “may you be made a pair” (a wish that they be paired/matched).
segments.s715-g73: فرستاده (the messenger) is the subject; آمد is the simple past 'came'. The prepositional phrase به نزدیک شاه follows the verb, with به + نزدیک meaning 'toward/near' the king—so literal order is 'came to near the king.' This is standard Classical/Persian word order where the verb can precede its locative phrase.
segments.s715-g74: بپیمود is an archaic/simple past form of پیمودن 'to traverse/cover' (3rd singular). The phrase به یک ماه کمتر is a time-measure phrase meaning 'in less than one month' (به + duration + کمتر). راه is the direct object 'the road/distance' and follows the verb; altogether 'he traversed the road in under a month.'
segments.s715-g75: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'. برخواند is past tense 'read aloud' or 'recited' (here 'read'). آن نامه را uses آن 'that' plus نامه 'letter' with the object marker را marking it as the definite object; شهریار (the king) appears at the end as the subject. The clause = 'When the king read that letter.'
segments.s715-g76: بپیچید is past 'turned' or 'turned aside' (from پیچیدن). The conjunction و links the next predicate ترسان شد 'became afraid'—ترسان is an adjectival participle 'afraid' and شد is the auxiliary/past 'became'. از introduces the cause 'because of / at' and روزگار here means 'the times' or 'fate.' The sequence describes two successive reactions: he turned away and became afraid of the times/fate.
segments.s715-g77: فرستاد is simple past 'sent' (he dispatched). The conjunction و links the clauses. ایرانیان را uses the plural noun 'Iranians' with the object marker را—here it indicates the people whom he summoned or to whom he read. بخواند is past 'read/recited' (here 'read aloud' to them), so the clause means 'He sent (a messenger) and read [the letter] to the Iranians.'
segments.s715-g78: سخن‌های is the plural construct 'the words/speeches/messages of…'; خاقان is the possessor 'the khagan.' سراسر is an adverb/adjective 'throughout' or 'entirely.' براند here is a past-form of راندن 'to drive' (3rd singular) used in a literary sense meaning 'dispersed/put out' or 'drove forth'—in context it conveys that the khagan's words were spread or issued everywhere. Grammatically this is a verb phrase with the subject understood from the prior clause.
segments.s715-g79: همان 'the same' modifies نامه 'letter.' بنمود is past 'showed' (he/they displayed). The conjunction و connects بنمود to برخواندند, which is past plural 'they read aloud' or 'they called/read aloud'—here it means they read the letter out loud. Word order places the object earlier (همان نامه) and the verbs after it.
segments.s715-g80: بزرگان is the subject 'the great ones' (elders/leaders). به اندیشه is a prepositional phrase 'into/with thought' (meaning 'in thought' or 'deep in thought'). درماندند is past plural 'were helpless' / 'were at a loss.' Together: 'The elders were at a loss in thought.'
segments.s715-g41: وگر نه is a conditional preface meaning 'but if not / otherwise'. فرستم is a first-person present/subjunctive (here future/intentional) verb 'I send / I will send'. In classical/poetic word order the verb precedes its noun phrase; ز ایران سپاه is a prepositional/object phrase 'an army from Iran' with poetic ز = 'from'.
segments.s715-g42: به توران sets the target/location ('to/towards Turan') and is followed by کنم, the first-person present/subjunctive of کردن used here as 'I will make / I will cause'. روز روشن سیاه is a compact poetic object: noun (روز) followed by adjectives روشن and سیاه — the sequence describes making the 'bright day' (روشن روز) into 'black' (سیاه); the verb کنَم takes this whole phrase as its object.
segments.s715-g43: چوآن is a temporal conjunction 'when/that' introducing the clause. نامه is the subject 'that letter'. نزدیک خاقان is an adverbial/locative 'near the khagan' (classical placement between subject and verb). رسید is the simple past third-person 'arrived/reached.'
segments.s715-g44: بران گونه گفتار is a nominal phrase meaning 'speech/manner of that kind' (بران = 'of that / upon that', گونه = 'kind/manner', گفتار = 'speech/words'), placed before the verb for emphasis. خسرو شنید is a straightforward past-clause 'Khosrow heard' with شنید as past tense verb taking the preceding phrase as its object; the word order (object phrase before subject+verb) is a common poetic/inverted arrangement.
segments.s715-g45: فرستاده را گفت is a reporting clause 'he said to the messenger' where را marks the definite/affected noun (here the addressee). گفت is the speech-verb introducing the following directive; فردا پگاه is a temporal adverbial 'tomorrow at dawn' that sets when the messenger should act.
segments.s715-g46: چو آیی بدر is a subordinate temporal clause 'when you come to Badr' with چو + آیی (second-person present/subjunctive) used to mean 'when you go/come'. پاسخ‌نامه is the object 'the reply-letter', and خواه is an archaic/elliptical verb form functioning here as a directive/optative (imperative-like) 'ask for / seek / fetch' — the clause is an indirect command introduced by گفت.
segments.s715-g47: فرستاده آمد is the simple past 'the messenger came.' دلی پر شتاب is an adjectival/participial phrase attached to the subject meaning 'with a heart full of haste' (دلی = 'a heart/heartfelt', پر = 'full (of)', شتاب = 'haste'); in Persian such descriptive phrases commonly follow the verb or the noun they qualify in poetry.
segments.s715-g48: نبد is an archaic/poetic negative-existence verb meaning 'there was not' (a variant of نبود). زان سپس is a temporal adverbial 'from that time on / thereafter'. جای آرام و خواب is a compound noun phrase 'place of rest and sleep' which serves as the thing that did not exist; the clause therefore reads 'from then on there was no place of rest and sleep.'
segments.s715-g17: گزاینده is an agent noun (active participle) used as a title: 'the one who strikes/avenges'. It is followed by a relative clause هرکه جوید بدی ('whoever seeks evil') where جوید is the present/classical form 'seeks' (not a subjunctive here but simple present/classical present) and بدی is its object. Word order is noun + relative clause, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s715-g18: فزاینده is another active participle used as a noun 'the increaser/augmenter'. It takes the noun phrase دانش ایزدی ('divine knowledge') in genitive/attribute order: دانش (knowledge) modified by ایزدی (divine). The whole phrase is a second epithet parallel to the previous segment.
segments.s715-g19: The line begins with the poetic preposition ز 'from' (here marking source/causation) applying to the coordinated nouns that follow. نادانی 'ignorance' is the first noun, followed by و 'and' and then دانش و راستي 'knowledge and truthfulness' — two coordinated attributes. The preposition before the list governs the whole series.
segments.s715-g20: The preposition ز is repeated for a new series: 'from scarcity (کمی) and crookedness/wrongness (کژی) and (از) from/ out of deficiency (کاستی)'. The second 'از' is a variant/preposition equivalent to ز and emphasizes the final item. Conjunction و links the nouns in the usual coordinate way.
segments.s715-g21: بیابی here is a classical present/subjunctive/optative form meaning 'you will find' or 'you may find'. چو is a conditional 'if' (classical usage), and گویی in context is the speech verb 'you say' (classical 2nd-person form), introducing the clause که یزدان یکیست ('that God is one'). که introduces the reported clause and یکیست is the copula 'is one'.
segments.s715-g22: ورا is a dative/locative 'to him/for him' (referring to God). یار 'companion' and وهمتا 'peer/equal' are coordinated with و; the following phrase و انباز نیست is negative existential: نیست 'does not exist'. انباز is an archaic/uncertain word here (treated as a noun 'anbaz' — no precise gloss in the supplied senses), so the clause says 'he has no companion or equal and no anbaz exists.'
segments.s715-g23: بیابد is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'he/one finds' (classical poetic usage). هر آنکس که is the restrictive relative 'every person who/one who', with که introducing the relative clause. نیکی بجست is a past action 'sought goodness' (بجست = sought), so the whole means 'everyone who sought goodness will find (it).' The main verb precedes the relative clause, a common classical order.
segments.s715-g24: مباد is an optative/negative-jussive 'may it not be' (a wish that something not happen). آنک is a relative/connector 'that (which)', linking to the clause او دست بد را بشست ('he washed the hand of the bad/wicked one'), where را marks دست بد as the definite direct object and بشست is past 'washed'. The clause expresses the wish 'may it not be that he washed the hand of the wicked.'

sentences.s715-s17: He came to your presence and you accepted him; when (you) took him by the hand amid the throng.
sentences.s715-s18: Would anyone of the righteous take this course? I am not of the same mind about this affair.
sentences.s715-s19: When they bring this letter near you, ponder it deeply — your counsel is dark.
sentences.s715-s20: If you bind that servant's feet and send him to us, you will become useful to us.
sentences.s715-s13: There was a certain bad servant of the king, ungrateful — he knew neither the chief nor God.
sentences.s715-s14: There was a certain insignificant, idle, and nameless man; his father raised him because the time had come.
sentences.s715-s15: His deeds are not hidden in the world, among the ancients and among the guests.
sentences.s715-s16: No one accepted him; he had no means — and if, in wisdom, he stood on the highest pedestal...
sentences.s715-s41: Thus they received an answer from the Iranians: 'These are the Farr, Orand, and the crown of the Kians.'
sentences.s715-s42: Do not take such matters lightly to heart; consult once with the wise old man.
sentences.s715-s43: In the letter write: do not treat such a matter lightly; do not darken this farr and the ancient lamp.
sentences.s715-s44: Choose from Iran one old man, wise and handsome, famed and a scribe.
sentences.s715-s1: Thus, when the reports reached Iran, they came to the king of the brave.
sentences.s715-s2: that to Bahram belong the kingship and the treasure; you have more of them — you have not borne hardship.
sentences.s715-s3: He became full of pain and sorrow because of his concern; his heart turned twisted because of his conduct.
sentences.s715-s4: He kept taking counsel with the grandees together; he spoke much and weighed the greater and the lesser.
sentences.s715-s45: Who comes here near the Khaghan, speaks, and listens to his counsel.
sentences.s715-s46: He relates who Bahram was on the first day and what he sought afterward in his heroics.
sentences.s715-s47: It continued until his affairs became straight; thereupon the Lord sought a servant.
sentences.s715-s48: If it is not made good in a month's work, time will take a whole year.
sentences.s715-s25: He kept on until he saw the radiant lamp; he ran toward the Chinese khagan's court.
sentences.s715-s26: The khagan then brought (or sent) a scribe as well, with a cloak, a pen, musk and Chinese silk.
sentences.s715-s27: In reply he wrote: “Secret praise — from me, a humble servant, to the Creator of the world.”
sentences.s715-s28: Then he said that he had read that letter and had seated the envoy before him.
sentences.s715-s49: When Bahram was the khaghan's son‑in‑law, it was not easy to speak ill of him.
sentences.s715-s50: One ought to speak well and sufficiently; one must not keep things secret so that anyone should know.
sentences.s715-s29: Do not speak such words to the servants; they are unbecoming of that ancient house.
sentences.s715-s30: They have not a scrap of the moon (i.e. any radiance) at all, nor do they know who holds that place.
sentences.s715-s31: All China and Turan are entirely mine; moreover Hital too rules by decree.
sentences.s715-s32: I would not, even for a moment, call a man an oath-breaker — do not tell me such tales.
sentences.s715-s5: On the dark night he commanded that he be made scribe; he turned the pen-nib into the point of an arrow.
sentences.s715-s6: He wrote (sent) a letter to the Chinese Khagan; you said that he made a pen out of his dagger.
sentences.s715-s7: First he offered praise to the Creator, the Powerful and the Wise, and to (the) Fate/Time.
sentences.s715-s8: Worthy of the sun, Saturn, and the moon, a signifying emblem of the king before the presence (the court).
sentences.s715-s33: If I take Bahram's hand in mine, then from that (moment) — through love — I shall be utterly undone within.
sentences.s715-s34: No judge will call/summon me by pure water; save for the Pure God, I have no fear.
sentences.s715-s35: If greatness were added to you, you might have greater wisdom than this evil.
sentences.s715-s36: Thereupon he placed a seal on the letter with affection and said that, “May you be made a pair.”
sentences.s715-s37: The messenger came up near the king and covered the road in less than a month.
sentences.s715-s38: When the king read that letter, he turned away and became frightened at the times.
sentences.s715-s39: He sent (someone) and read it to the Iranians; he spread the khagan’s words throughout.
sentences.s715-s40: They showed the same letter and read it aloud, and the great men were at a loss in thought.
sentences.s715-s21: But if not, I will send an army from Iran and make Turan's bright day turn black.
sentences.s715-s22: When that letter reached the khagan, Khosrow heard speech of that sort.
sentences.s715-s23: He said to the messenger, 'Tomorrow at dawn, when you come to Badr, ask for (or fetch) the reply-letter.'
sentences.s715-s24: The messenger came with a heart full of haste; from then on there was no place for rest or sleep.
sentences.s715-s9: The punisher of whoever seeks evil, the augmenter of divine knowledge.
sentences.s715-s10: From ignorance and from knowledge and truthfulness; from scarcity and crookedness and from deficiency.
sentences.s715-s11: You will find — if you say that God is One — that to Him there is no companion or equal, nor does any anbaz exist.
sentences.s715-s12: Every person who sought goodness will find it; may it never be that he washed the hand of the wicked.
